sous_titres.text: I'm John Dykstra.
Here today with me are Scott Stokdyk and Anthony LaMolinara.
Anthony was director of animation.
Scott's the visual effects supervisor for SPI. Imageworks.
And I am the visual effects designer.
This title sequence was done by Imaginary Forces.
The important part of the relationship between the people in visual effects...
...and the people who were doing the title sequence was obviously the web.
We worked with them to come up with a web that gave the impression...
...of the web that's in the movie, but keep the stylized quality...
...that our director wanted to have for the title sequence.
Some of the volumes that you see here...
...the Spider-Man character, the hand, the Goblin's face...
...was interpreted from the geometry that we used for our virtual character.
They simplified it and put it into the sequence.
They were trying for something that wasn't real.
They wanted a stylized version of the web and the characters.
They succeeded.
- So we shared. There you are. - Yeah, there I am. That's me.
- "Visual effects designed by." - Yeah.
We shared a couple models, passed some models over.
I hope we get to do the next one.
- The next set of titles. - The next opening sequence.
It's interesting, because it's this sort of period graphic...
...that goes on the front of a movie that is fairly starkly real.
If we did these as photo-realistic webs for this whole sequence...
...it would have taken months.
This is the transition from our stylized web into the real web.
The opening scene of the film.
The background for this was shot in New York...
...and they overlaid the synthetic web from the title sequence...
...across the cross-dissolve to create the opening sequence.
Originally, wasn't it...
...a cross-dissolve behind her head, where the bus materialized?
There were a couple of different ideas of introducing the opening sequence...
...introducing Mary Jane, whom the story is about...
...as is said in the monologue.
But this was the...
This was chosen as the best means to make that transition...
...because it didn't leave us looking at her for a huge amount of time.
- Don't even think about it. - You're so lame, Parker.
That's a good look for Tobey with those glasses.
And like that.
We shot all of this stuff...
Throughout the film, we used live-action locations in New York.
In some cases we shot in Los Angeles to be substituted for New York.
And sometimes we created components completely synthetically...
...that are added into both locations in Los Angeles and New York...
...to create our version of New York City.
- Can we drive around the corner? - Why?
The entrance is right there.
Dad, this is public school. I can't show up in a Rolls.
I should trade my car for a Jetta...
Lot of setup here. Lot of exposition.
One of the things that we're gonna talk about...
Working with Sam was terrific. He's a very collaborative director.
Using his version of the script...
...the script that he approved and interpreted to us...
...we started creating the preliminary visual effects for this movie.
Sam not only welcomed...
...contributions from people on the crew...
...he encouraged it.
He was the arbiter of all the stuff used in the film, as the director...
...but everybody got the feeling that they had a vested interest in this...
...in some very small part as a filmmaker...
...as well as whatever their particular job was.
I think that comes through in the sense of this movie.
I think it trickled on down through the whole crew.
Our CG supervisors, Ken Hahn, Peter Nofz and Dan Eaton...
...they at different points had input into the process, which was nice.
It's unreal.
Getting close to our first effects shot. Any minute now.
The atomic spider.
One of the things about this story is it's a period piece.
In the original origin, the character's bitten by an irradiated spider.
We now know, if you get bitten by an irradiated spider...
...you'll probably get radiation poisoning.
But in those days it imbued the character with superhuman capabilities.
Rather than use a sort of old saw for this superhero transition...
...Sam brought the story into a contemporary vein...
...by using the DNA basis.
Genetics, this genome-splicing capability...
...as the component to cause the transition from our normal kid...
...to the superhero character, Spider-Man.
That contemporary science brings the film into a contemporary sense.
When she describes the characteristics of all these spiders...
...we think about what Spider-Man will do with those powers.
It'll be a spider trapped in a man's body...
...dying to get out.
Over five painstaking years, Columbia's research facility...
- You gonna talk to her now? - Oh, no. Come on.
That's part of how Spider-Man moves.
His molecular structure has been changed after this bite...
...so that he no longer moves according to the physical rules we know...
...with human bodies, but something combined between human and spider.
- Like a Spider-Man? - Just like that.
One of the things I point out in the live-action material:
The personality of these characters...
...both Tobey and Willem...
...they are the basis...
...for our hero and our villain.
Their postures, positions and body language...
...are an important part of defining them as a character.
Keeping an eye on that, trying to integrate that into the characters...
...once they are masked and dressed in costume...
...was a very important part of making the character seem...
...like he was an extension of the actor...
...as we have seen him without the costume.
Here's Tobey getting ready to take his photo.
You did a lot of photography a long time ago, didn't you, John?
Oh, yeah. That was me, actually.
I got bitten by a spider. It bit me in my visual-effects lobe.
Trying to get the girls by taking their picture.
I did fashion photography when I was in college. It was terrific.
You do a headsheet.
- There's our first effects shot. - There you go.
- The beautiful crawling spider. - That pesky spider.
A close-up is coming up, which was a lot harder on many levels.
That was a real spider, and...
Stand by.
This guy let the spider crawl on his hand. It isn't Tobey.
The guy who did the spider shot quit.
This bite is so short. It was a much longer shot...
...and we just see a little snippet of the actual bite.
It actually sits on his hand and grinds into his fangs for a while.
Parker, let's do it.
To get that close-up of that hand that the close-up spider was on...
- John, you want to talk about that? - Yes, I'd be pleased to.
First matte painting.
The close-up of the hand was done with a very special piece of optics...
...designed for photo-microscopy...
...which is microscopic photography.
We used special strobe lights to illuminate the hand...
...to give us enough brightness to make the entire hand be in relative focus.
It was recorded at high speed to out down on the quivering of the hand...
...so we could put our CG spider on there.
I talked over the guy landing the glider.
We were just looking at our first effects.
There's a practical glider, but we added effects.
We added heat distortion, thrusters coming out the bottom...
And heat distortion.
One thing that's interesting:
Sam's concept for this movie was for it to be real.
It's not Metropolis. It's not Gotham. It's New York...
...right down to the pigeon droppings and the rust underneath the pipes.
Throughout the film, he'd ask us to pursue...
...a very "real" effect in all of these environments.
The toughest thing to do when we made virtual versions...
...of the characters and the objects was putting in high-frequency noise.
By that, I mean the dirt and debris...
...that populates our real life.
Dust bunnies under the bed, stains on the wall...
...the pigeon droppings on the windowsill.
That's tough for a computer to do because it's random, a chaotic event.
So although we were successful...
Scott can talk about the procedures...
...but we had our artists go back in and add dirt to many sequences...
...to give you the visual cues that this is real, not computer-generated.
A lot of texture work on this movie. Many thousand textures.
We figured out there were 62,000 textures painted.
A texture is a...?
It's basically just a color map that's mapped onto objects...
...and represents its texture, if you will.
It represents its look...
Like taking the textures from Tobey's face...
...and laying them on the model of Tobey's face...
...to achieve the look and try to make it look realistic.
- The texture is basically a photograph. - Starts out as a photograph.
A photograph of the surface you want to reproduce...
...in the computer environment.
The artists add to it...
...and then that photograph could be shrink-wrapped...
...or vacuum-formed onto the shape, which we call the geometry...
...of the person or the object that we're creating.
Since the photograph contains all of that detail...
...you get that in your representation.
This is not Tobey Maguire's body.
But he'd love to have it.
It was Tobey's head mapped onto that body.
It was a big tracking job. And color-correction job too.
In this recombination lab...
We did some digital color-timing on many of these sequences.
When you take original photography and bring it into the computer...
...you have to make adjustments to the color...
...to make sure when you take it out of the computer...
...it will look like the original photography.
This is a nice matte painting. The OSCORP image.
Done by Ivo, one of our matte painters.
The image from the computer needs to cut back into the movie...
...adjacent to original negatives.
Rather than make it significantly different from the original photography...
...we try and make it match the original photography...
...so when they do color correction, everything falls into place.
But in many sequences, especially with cross-dissolves and fades...
...you have scene-to-scene color timing that's critical.
So we did an awful lot of digital color adjustment...
...throughout those montage sequences.
I was wrong before. Ivo, the head of our matte department...
...painted the daytime OSCORP lab exterior...
...and Dave Bleisch painted the nighttime version.
Norman Osborn is put into this thing, this dramatic moment...
...and he goes, "It's cold!" when he straps him in.
I think that's pretty interesting.
We're coming up to the green gas modification shots.
They didn't want to shoot with toxic green gas, for some reason.
I don't understand it.
So our friends at Digiscope did a handful of shots...
...maybe 10 to 15 color-correction shots on the gas.
Started out as white smoke and got turned into green.
Norman?
One of the things Anthony talked about earlier was...
...characters going from being human to superhuman.
One of the things that people talked about...
...was whether or not we used motion capture for our characters.
Although I have an opinion, Anthony's might differ.
I think motion capture's an interesting technique...
...to get the detail that defines a person's body language.
Motion capture is essentially a series of measurement points attached...
...to a person's body.
You have that person go through a series of moves...
...and then you record those points.
It allows you to then animate a synthetic version of that character.
When we did our motion capture, we got great high-frequency detail...
...in terms of defining a character's personality.
But if you have a character jump off a three-foot-high box...
...that high-frequency detail gives away...
...that all he did was jump off a box.
So the animators have to expand the personality of this leap...
...into something for a 100-foot fall.
Although the motion capture for high-frequency detail was very good...
...that same detail was a giveaway...
...when we made him do superhuman things.
That's Tobey's real body, by the way.
Superhuman.
As a result, the principal characters, Goblin and Spider-Man...
...were key-frame animated...
To use the high-frequency detail...
...we had to do so much adjustment...
...it would take longer to do it that way than to animate it from the top.
Another thing about motion capture...
...as the technology exists right now, it is cumbersome.
You have to dress up in a Lycra suit...
...and have these big, reflective balls stuck all over you.
You can't go outside a 30-by-30 area.
There's a lot of new technology that's being developed.
Hopefully, it'll be a little less restrictive...
...in how you capture the data, and the setup for it.
Not that it'll necessarily influence our choice of doing motion capture.
I always tell people how pissed off the gorilla got in Hollow Man...
- ...when we stuck those balls on him. - And shaved him. Or her.
We missed it. There were two beautiful wire removals there.
Him flying out, the doctor flying out, and Willem flying out.
As far as motion capture goes...
...even if, say, a guy from Cirque du Soleil...
...could swing down between the buildings...
...the technology's not there to capture that in that distance.
Yeah, it's too big. When you compress that action into a small space...
...it looks compressed into a small space.
So the accuracy of the capture is also a curse.
If you don't use a real actor you can tell just because of the way he moves.
Anything Spider-Man would do...
...shooting a web one way, then changing directions...
The G-force on his body...
...if it didn't rip his arm out, would rip his whole body in half.
It's impossible for a person to do...
...and therefore impossible for a real person to fake.
Tobey looking at his hands is a precursor...
...to the close-up of his hands that comes up.
Dad, are you all right?
- Dad's been out for a fun night. - Exactly.
A couple of other things, just definitions.
One, we decided that the Spider-Man character wanted to be...
...a mechanism by which the audience experiences...
...what it's like to swing between buildings.
At the end of the film, Sam wanted the audience...
...to feel they had been Spider-Man.
The animators worked with a camera as well as the character...
...much like a skydiving team works.
The guy doing the performance is photographed...
...by his partner, the skydiving photographer.
We're gonna see some webs in a minute.
Lot of web shots. One of our guys...
This is a mechanical rig, where he catches all this stuff.
Yeah. He actually did that.
Pretty good. Take 156.
At any rate...
...we decided that Spider-Man, when he swung through the city...
...would have his own cameraman with him.
The cameraman had the same abilities as Spider-Man...
...and was able to operate the camera.
The cameraman gets better over the course of the film as does Spider-Man.
He gets more adept at doing the photography.
That's another way we added this sense of reality to things.
This is a practical... The first one's a practical web.
That's a real one, and then this...
That last one's a CG.
The first one was our effects shot. That's practical, and then...
I can't even tell anymore.
Our guy, Theo, set up this great web system...
...including the dynamics and shading, just to be multipurposed.
To work for small webs, and to work for the large-scale webs he swings on.
This fight scene coming up, as well as Wirework that's in this...
There is a wire attached, lifting him up in the air...
...or supporting him so he can lean back and not lose his balance.
A lot of these shots were speeded up in certain sections of this shot...
...to give them more force, like the punch.
John should talk about this.
Well, this is spider sense.
It's made up of all of the components that you want to see...
...when you think about what Spider-Man can do.
It's expanded time. Why don't you talk about the fight?
We took part of the punch and sped it up so that there's more force...
...and slowed it down at the right moment so it's not so obvious.
And the one long shot where we see that arm just glide by him...
...and Tobey sees everything in slow motion.
That's another idea of the spider sense.
He's in one temporal realm, they're all in a different one.
And here, for example, when he grabs Flash's arm.
It's speeded up, then back to real speed as he holds it...
...and then it's speeded up again for the punch.
Which one?
There were so many shots that were sped up and slowed down.
Both Stan and Bob liked experimenting with that.
- We tried a little bit of it too. - All right.
Spider sense, by its very definition, is time expansion.
Spider-Man slows everything down to inspect all these potential threats.
The beginning of this, where you saw the fly being inspected...
...were events that he had no control over.
His spider sense, which is newly developed, checked out all this stuff.
It gives you a sense of his ineptitude.
Next is a sequence where you see his first experimentation...
...with his newfound powers.
That's the spider's genetic modification to his wrist.
It's not exactly where the spinneret ought to be, but...
We figured putting it in the right place might be a mistake.
There's the big microscopic hair shot.
- This is how he climbs up the wall. - That was Theo's shot too.
A lot of tight tracking work to get those to work too.
Here are our first real digital environment shots, actually.
They're extension shots. That's a fake alley floor there.
And there.
When we look up on the side, it's all building extensions.
This was the beginning of the personality for this camera.
The idea that the camera actually rose with him, kind of stayed with him.
Keeping the camera with the character, as opposed to watching him from afar.
Most of this is Wirework.
It's our video-game shots.
We were going to use a stuntman, but they decided...
...that it was not practical to do. It was a little too dangerous.
Here's the first webs that he shoots.
He's figuring how to control his webs...
...and how to dictate where they go.
Up, up and away, web.
So the web, this is Theo's work...
...has a huge range of personalities.
It comes out of his wrist, it's thin and willowy in some cases.
It's big and strong in others.
It has to spread into an actual spider web in some cases...
...and in other cases it's a lump on somebody's face.
An enormous effort went into defining the web.
If you think about real webs, the biggest problem is, they're invisible.
If bugs could see the webs, they wouldn't fly into them.
We knew that, in order to meet the expectations of an audience...
...who knows of spider webs as just glints...
...we had to figure out a way to make the web like a spider web...
...in that it had what we called a sundog defining it by highlight...
...and keep enough volume to feel it was strong enough...
...to do whatever it was we were asking the web to do.
There are a number of practical webs intercut with our CG ones.
The web had to be flexible enough to look practical...
...or go away from that when we needed to.
That last shot where he was swinging on the web and hits the wall...
We'll see him almost do the same thing when he's in his homemade spidey suit.
Then he just misses it at the last moment.
Gotten a little better.
We've got this character arc.
He's not so facile when he first gains that capability.
Over the course of the film, he gets better and better.
You see that happen. Also, the way he is followed through the film...
The camera work also gets better and better.
So everybody's kind of got this same learning curve.
I guess you can always hear us.
Everybody shouts.
Your aunt and uncle don't.
When we were shooting this stuff...
...you see these scenes that last two or three minutes...
...yet it takes an entire night to shoot this stuff.
This was a situation where color correction was used.
Over the course of the evening, we went from dusk to night to dawn.
And it wasn't shot in continuity.
In some cases, they're taking light out of a scene.
In other cases, they're adding light to make it all consistent throughout.
A difficult problem when he was on the roof...
...is that the actual size it needs to be to see it in the distance...
...is gigantic as opposed to what it really would be.
Yet I buy it when I see it on screen.
The illusion in the service of the story.
It's cinemagraphic license, making the web change size.
Also, depending on the background...
...we had to do different things if it went over a bright sky.
The whiter specular hits wouldn't show up as much.
You had to get some dark in there, and sundogs running down too.
The web had several different states. It's liquid when shot from his wrist.
As it attached to things, it did a catalyzation...
...which was a change of state, like water becoming ice.
When it changed state, there was a difference to the visual personality.
You can see when it strikes a building when he swings through the city...
...the web has a transition that occurs once it's stuck to a surface.
He also seems, through some psycho-physio process...
...to make the web come out and have different consistencies.
One time it would be hard as steel, another it would be elastic...
...and he could pull on it and pop himself up to a building.
Just like a mutant spider could, I'm sure.
Absolutely.
Hey, M.J.! Come take a ride in my new birthday present.
Come on.
I gotta go.
Something I'd like to do in the sequel is put Tobey's idiosyncrasies...
...into the digital actor.
There's another alleyway. Lots of alleyways in this movie.
Cool car.
John was talking about the performance of the digital actor.
In this film, we're almost working backward.
Usually you work from the facial expression down...
...as far as the hierarchy of what gives the expression.
In this case, there is no facial expression.
Spider-Man maybe has a jaw moving. Goblin has nothing.
It's a very big challenge...
...to try to let this digital actor...
...emote from body movement.
Goblin is certainly more complex than Spider-Man...
...with his Shakespearean moves and psychotic personality...
...that goes from kind to sadistic.
That was a very big challenge, something we'll develop more next time.
The interesting thing about the personality of this character...
Sam asked, and maybe you can give input...
...that we use some of the classic Spider-Man poses...
...to brand this character from the comic books.
I know the animators had that stuff as reference.
Do you have a sense of how you integrated that stuff?
Certainly, we worked from all of the comics...
...then tried to figure out what pose works best in a given situation.
If he's perched, waiting for something.
In the last swing, there are a lot of...
...typical key Spider-Man poses.
When his hands are raised over his head...
...his wrists are arched and his fingers are in a formation.
That's a good example of these poses.
It comes from the storyboard artist and animators...
...on up through me, you, Sam...
...and everybody else who's watching this film on a daily basis.
The other interesting thing... You talked about Willem Dafoe.
When we started defining what the Goblin would be like as a character...
...we started with this conveyance, the Goblin glider.
We built a simulator, a motion base with a glider attached.
It boiled down to something like a mechanical bull...
...only you had to stand on it. It was an ankle-breaker.
We worked with the stunt people to figure out a way...
...to come up with something to give them a timing choreography...
...so when it moved, they'd anticipate it...
...and position themselves to look as if they're affecting the machine.
Interestingly, we weren't successful with the stunt people...
...but when Willem Dafoe showed up and started to define the character...
...they stuck him on the machine, and within 15 minutes of riding...
...he didn't put new moves in, he's just reacting to the machine...
...he managed to come up with these terrific moves for the Goblin.
I don't know how you feel about it, Anthony, but for me...
...when I saw him on the machine, I was encouraged...
...as to how well the character was going to work on the glider.
That was the first experience...
...of seeing the actor define the character for us...
- ...to bring to the screen. - Go ahead.
This sequence is interesting for color correction...
...because their skin tones were so different.
Ben had a more ruddy, reddish complexion.
Tobey leaned toward olive tones and went green, if you weren't careful.
Especially in two-shots, where they're together. This was tricky.
I remember when Willem came in.
The pumpkin bombs pop out of the glider...
...and when he had his mask on, he couldn't see where they came from.
He had to learn how to catch the bombs just by feel...
...or having his hand in the right place.
He ended up doing the work that a stuntman would normally do.
He dedicated himself to creating body-language personality for Goblin.
He was so studious in terms of his positions in every situation...
...as to whether or not it was appropriate for the Goblin.
A lot of effects guys tried to go in and be extras in the movie.
One guy, Jeff Wolverton, made it into this match...
...holding a Bone Saw sign.
- Is that him in the bikini? - Yeah, that's him on the left.
I haven't spotted him, but I'm going to go frame-by-frame on the DVD.
It's the guy trying to pitch a film.
The thing I didn't realize the first couple times I saw this movie...
...is that it's Bruce Campbell in a cameo as the announcer.
Bruce is the hero from Sam's movies.
And you're participating under your own free will?
Down the hall to the ramp. May God be with you. Next.
Here's a case where it's a reverse of the norm.
Usually, they couldn't do what they needed to do with a stuntman...
...so we did it digitally.
In this sequence...
...they did most everything with the actor or a stuntman.
Wouldn't you like to have done Bone Saw CG, though?
That would've been fun.
$3000 will be paid to...
- What's your name, kid? - The Human Spider.
That's it? That's the best you got?
- Yeah. - Oh, that sucks.
The sum of $3000 will be paid to...
...the terrifying, the deadly...
There's some web work coming up in this sequence...
...and it's more of an elastic-type web.
Anthony mentioned the different characteristics.
In this sequence, Tobey uses...
...that web as just a very springy substance to help him do some flips.
Almost like bungee cord.
This is another place, although they did this actual work on-stage...
...this is another case where the Cirque du Soleil performers...
...or performances were used.
The idea of the multiple webs sort of like some of the stuff...
...that they do on multiple bungee cords in Cirque du Soleil performances.
Cage!
There were some speed up of some shots in here too.
Some skip-framing, or different software sometimes...
...to speed it up and make it look a little smoother.
...Please lock the cage doors at this time.
This is some kind of mistake. I didn't sign up for a cage match!
Unlock the thing! Take the chain off!
Hey, freak show!
You're going nowhere.
I got you for three minutes.
Three minutes of playtime.
When we did tests of our homemade costume...
...we used some of this photography...
...to check how our CG version costume would light.
We really liked the dramatic lighting here.
And it made for a good color comparison kind of check.
Smash him!
One of the things that...
Back to this Green Goblin thing we were talking about before...
One thing we did to make the Green Goblin feel...
...as if he were a contemporary character...
...is the motions of him on his glider.
Rather than looking like he's standing on top of a small airplane...
...and flying straight and level...
...we gave him the sense of motion you get...
...from skateboarding and surfing and snowboarding.
That kind of finesse that the characters have in movement...
...you see in sports to bring this into a contemporary vein as well.
Yeah, the Goblin's glider is sort of like his pet.
Some kind of pet that he can call at will...
...and watches out for him, and comes back to him.
We had to give the feeling like he controlled it...
...not completely by body movement. A thought process was going on...
...as if he had to just lean and it knew where to go, as opposed to...
...just pushing it around by leaning.
It was partially the understanding of the Goblin and him as one.
- What the hell...? - Put the money in the bag.
Hurry UP!
Hey! He stole the gate!
Stop that guy!
Stop him! He's got my money!
I like this costume. It had interesting detail work...
...when we made our CG version.
One of the first things we did were the shoes...
...which you don't really see here.
But he had these red Nike sneakers we sort of did on a turntable...
...flying around a screen to try to balance...
...how the colors and lighting looked on it.
The interesting thing about a movie like this...
...because it took two years, we had to make promises...
...we weren't certain that we could fulfill at the beginning of this film.
As any film of this scope, it's a living document.
It has to be able to change over the course of time.
The homemade costume was something...
...that evolved over the course of time.
The homemade costume wasn't in the first script...
...certainly not as it ended up being in the film.
And this is something that we had to embrace.
It was different than our hero character costume:
It's baggy and floppy and has folds and the wind affects it.
So it was actually more challenging...
...than the more polished, finished version of Spider-Man.
Definitely. We had to build an extra layer, basically...
...on top of our slick costume and do a cloth simulation.
John Lee did that and had to add in...
...all the effects where he's flying and get those ripples...
...and the Wind's amplitude and frequency...
...to just give him a sense of motion.
Multilayers of wrinkles, wrinkling in there.
You can see on this sweatshirt how many wrinkles and folds there are.
This is a little thicker than our costume...
...but those kind of details really give it realism.
The guys who did the cloth came up with a garment workers' stamp...
- ...they put on dailies. - Migrant textile workers of Imageworks.
An interesting thing about this cloth:
When you make these costumes, you end up using the same techniques...
...real patternmakers use.
So this cloth you see here was fitted to the body of the character.
If it doesn't fit right in the chest, it might come back and take a pleat.
We re-tailor it, basically.
And it's actually tailoring the way a real tailor works.
That wall crawl is a great shot by Spencer Cook.
It shows what we came to for the character's movement.
First we tried suspending him from a track...
...and let him crawl along the surface, A stuntman or Tobey.
But there's no way you can get the correct weight distribution.
You lift one hand, they've got two feet and one arm...
...the way that pulls on the body, it didn't look right.
Here's the shot when he just misses the wall.
The camera follows him.
In one of those shots we had a bus...
...and on top there's lettering. We put "PT13" on there.
PT13 was just one of our first building tests. It was an homage to that.
The thing about this fabric in this sequence...
I think I heard you talking about this, Anthony.
The fabric enhances the sense of motion.
The idea that it's fluttering helps the animators...
...keep a sense of motion in the character.
The fabric gives an impression of the wind moving.
It makes the animation... It complements it.
It makes you aware of how much clothing...
...is what tells you the kind of movement a person is making.

This one.
He's animated, funny enough.
The rolling bear animation.
We did quite a few different versions of that.
That must have been an interesting thing to animate.
Indirect animation. You animate the character and then cast his shadow.
Because you've got to go by silhouettes.
What looks right from one angle doesn't look right from another.
Stop that guy!
You had a version of the character you were using...
...and he was in an impossible pose to make a feasible-looking shadow.
It wasn't our final physiquing on the character...
...so his shape wasn't perfect. You had to manipulate that a bit.
That was some of Koji's earliest physiquing there.
Part of an eight-month process.
"Physiquing" being the creation of the model of the character.
Actually, the Goblin and Spider-Man were set up by two different people...
...and set up in different ways.
Koji's version, Koji Morihiro, set up Spider-Man.
He approaches it differently...
...than JJ Blumenkrantz did on Goblin.
One of our guys, Alberto, helped set up the Goblin system...
...which was based on an underlying skeleton and muscle system.
The Goblin also had, in addition to having a skin over Willem's skin...
...it had a hard exoskeleton that had a little bit of flux in it...
...but basically, like, slid...
...slid up and down his arms and his chest.
So it had to be a different technique than our Spider-Man.
The definition of the physical form of the peoples' bodies...
...also included a muscle system.
So when they flexed, you could see a change displacement...
...in the forms of their body.
The legs, the contours of their thighs and chests and arms.
There was also breathing. Anthony had access to breathing controls.
Those are the kinds of subtle details that tell the audience...
...that this is a live character, as opposed to a CG simulation.
A lot of that work was based on reference footage...
...we took of the actors and stunt doubles at the start.
We put them through calisthenics and range-of-motion tests...
...and basically took up days of their time to get all of that set up work.
But it paid off. I mean, just having video reference...
...of how the quads flex, and the deltoids and biceps.
All through the body, how muscles distort their volumes.
It was really useful.
And it had to be there because when he's in that custom-made costume...
...that's the only real feeling you get of this character being alive.
Because the suit is tight, there's no clothing to flow...
...and give the sense of movement.
So it's more difficult to bring the character to life in that costume.
I keep thinking of that shot in the fire building later...
...where Spencer animated him landing.
It's just such a nice, subtle flex in his quads on his legs as he lands.
It didn't make the out, but it gave a sense of the weight of the landing.
When we first started doing the work on this movie...
...we did a test as a proof of concept.
And we used a model of a body that came from another environment.
It wasn't the real character, but we put a Spider-Man costume on him...
...and had him climb up the side of a set piece, a piece of set wall.
And when we did the test, we shot the set wall with a camera move...
...with a stuntman crawling on his belly...
...using a rig we'd designed to let him slide on the surface.
The mechanical effects guys put that together.
Then we shot the same camera move without the character.
Then we took the version of the wall without the character on it...
...and created a CGI version using this existing model of this person.
Then Anthony and the guys... Did Spencer animate that?
- That first test? - Yeah.
Anyway, so we took this in and showed it to them.
I don't know who the lighters were. It was our first...
That was a long time ago.
- I think Greg Anderson was involved. - So we gave it our best shot.
This was before we'd worked out how we were going to do this.
We took it in, and the producer, sort of as a tongue-in-cheek joke...
...told the executives who were watching the film:
"So here's Tobey climbing up the wall."
And they ran film. The executives watched it.
The lights come up, and they smile and say:
"It's great-looking, but that couldn't have been Tobey. It's a stuntman."
So they had mistaken the animated, computer-generated character...
...that animators had put on the wall...
...and that SPI had lit and composited, for a real image.
So we knew we had a shot at being successful when they decided...
...our first test was indistinguishable from a real human being.
You look back on those tests now...
...and Spider-Man looks nothing like he does now in terms of his costume...
...and even some of his body language.
On every movie we do a test on, you look back...
...and that test looks so different than the final product.
We were trying to arrive in these tests...
...at the proper movement for him to be a mix of man and spider.
That anecdote of Laura Ziskin watching the screening and saying:
"Wow. You know, this character looks like a man...
...but, well, like a spider."
Everybody said: "That's exactly what it is."
And then we knew we had more or less what we needed to sell this character.

As it came up, it would slow down and steady out...

The process is interesting. You shoot all this material...
...and they edit the thing together.
There's times when complete sequences get left out.
And you end up with a sequence shot...
...for a particular time of day used at a different time.
Or a shot even where a shot that was made with the sun in one direction...
...cut with the sun in another direction, or perhaps the sun gone.
Those are things now, although they've always been a part of it...
...those are things now that we are using...
...digital color timing and techniques to smooth out...
...so they don't create as big a disconnect for the audience.
The trick is to get people to not be aware of being in a theatre.
The one thing we would never do though, is flip a shot.

You're still sensitive about your Times Square shot they flipped.
The whole process, every studio, every group...
...every visual effects house has a different approach...
...to how they're going to work together.
Even within a certain house, you'll find that every group...
...certainly every effects designer, is gonna have a different way of working.
On this, we were all together. All departments saw dailies together.
And I think that was healthy because everybody contributes.
It may hurt to hear what they have to say, but it usually makes it better.
We would separate animation dailies...
...where we were blocking out the shots with lighting and compositing dailies.
John, Anthony and I, and some supervisors would sit in all of them.

So we've seen this film many, many times over.
The little shot where Spider-Man's on the ground...

This character is a superhuman...
...but we still wanted him to be a thinking character.
So if everything's too well-choreographed...

As if the character weren't thinking on his feet at that moment.
It can look scripted, as it were. Contrived.
Interesting thing about this character, something Sam said consistently:
"I want to know what he's thinking."
When you have a character who has a face...
...the idea of establishing his thoughts is much easier...
...than with a character who is only using his body because he's masked.
That's the interesting thing about animation in this film.
From my point of view, you have a sense of what he's thinking...
...just based on the way his body's moving.
The actors actually dealt with the same issues as our animators.
Acting with the body instead of the face.
We both kind of had to come to our solutions separately.

He moves his body much less.

The tough thing about this sequence is it's a daylight exterior.
Without getting into specifics, this was shot in New York.
Portions of it were shot in New York, portions of it were shot on a stage.
Portions of it were shot in Downey, portions were computer-generated.
It's not necessarily the same piece all the time.
When you watch the sequence you can see...
...that we had to create everything from very small-scale...
...to very large-scale components to make this sequence complete.
There's no other way we could do it, aside from shutting down Times Square.
They talked to us... We talked to them, excuse me.
We talked to them about shutting down Times Square for the weekend.
They weren't going for it.
The animation on the balloons in there is dynamics...
...which is a method of...
...how the energy would flow through an object...
...if it were animated or hit with energy.
So in this case, the balloons are hit with air, or an object.
Dan Kramer did a nice job making these balloons look natural for their size...
...and the way it would move.
The balloons helped tie different environments together.
They gave good foreground objects and bridged the gap...
...between the different photography.
We have our somewhat controversial sign replacements in this sequence.
One thing the balloon did, in foreground and middle ground...
If you look at Times Square, we were on Dick Clark's balcony...
...the one he stands on during the New Year's celebration.
Then the people were below us.
If you stand and look down, everything's in the distance.
You don't have much of a sense of perspective or scale.
Putting the balloons into the sequence gave us some middle-ground elements...
...which allowed us to show not only the scale of the environment...
...but also to keep the screen alive with movement.
Because as the camera moves, you can see that the balloons...
...give a sense of foreground and background...
...which you wouldn't have if it wasn't for them.
These are a series of multi-purpose pumpkin bombs.

People got tired of coming.
And the crowds in the streets are all made up...

This is Peter Nofz's sequence, as a CG supervisor.
He coordinated the set up of a bunch of these different systems...
...including our crowd system and the character setup.
He got a taste of every pipeline.
Our building pipeline, our painted tile pipeline.
We stitched together photography that John shot in New York.
It came together.
Just for the sense of description, when Scott talks about a pipeline...
...it's the process characters and environments go through to be finished.
Once principle photography is done, if that's a component of a shot...
...let's say a background plate, in the case of this New York piece...
...then all of the pieces begin to come together.
Those pieces may take five weeks to assemble, or a week to assemble.
We call the length of time that it takes...
...to go from the beginning components to its completion a "pipeline."
In some cases the pipeline is tied to a character.
In other cases, it's tied to the complexity of the shot.
The last shot in the movie took six months, I believe.
- Yeah, six to eight months. - That's not a pure pipeline...
...but it gives you a sense of how long it takes.
We had to decide what we were actually going to do fairly early on...
...to get the shots done by the time the film was in the theatres.
By the time we got into really finishing...
...or getting close to finishing the last swing shot...
...everybody wanted to do more shots like it.
We just knew we didn't have the time in the schedule to do more.
Well, you use every moment.
I have yet to work on a movie where we said:
"We're done. We'll take the week off."
You always work until, as Sam said for this movie...
...I believe he's quoting someone else but he says:
"They will pry the film from his cold, dead hands."
That's pretty much the way these movies always are.
The whole process, from script to storyboard artists...
...to Sam, and then over to us...
...then going through you, John, then filtering through other people.
Going from me to the animators, and then back in the other direction again.
It goes back and forth through this hierarchy...
...and you arrive at something until Sam finally agrees upon it.
But everybody has their input into these things.
And different people input on many different things.
The lighter can comment about the animation.
An animator might comment about the lighting.
It works a little...
- Is that a sore spot? - No. Not at all.
Anthony and I always argue a little bit in jest, in dailies.
- But we won't do that now. - Oh, I'll miss it.
I was gonna say, the pre-visualization that Anthony was talking about...
...it's different for live-action shots and an all-CG shot.
For live-action photography, a lot of it was used to hash out...
...where the camera should be and what lens would be used...
...and to block out for the day of the shooting.
In the case of all-CG shots, it was actually much easier, in a way.
The animators were really blocking out the shot in that case.
It just went from pre-vis to finishing animation stages...
...straight into lighting without a photography section in that path.
We talked about pre-visualization...
...which is the geometry of a set or a location...
...and simple geometry of the characters...
...with a virtual camera that can be positioned to create shots.
We did pre-visualization on shots that were live-action pieces, as well...
...so that when we showed up on-stage to do the photography...
...we could tell the director of photography the focal lengths...
...and the distance from the camera to the subject.
That was a place to begin. Pre-visualization isn't cast in stone.
It's always, again, a living document.
But it gave everyone an exhibit to work with...
...to determine the needs of a shot on a given day.
It makes you more efficient. You put more of your money on screen...
...instead of putting the money into doing fix-its or changing your mind.
Pre-visualization was good.
John Schmidt was great about working out those things for shoot days.
He also made printouts for them.
He went through a ream of paper making printouts used for shooting.
Here comes the Goblin.

We used the pre-visualization as the exhibit that we discussed together.
It allowed us to bring the proper tools for the day's photography...
...as well as the postproduction work that had to be done in visual effects.

There's a lot of little wire removal and some composite shots...
...Imageworks didn't do. We worked with vendors.
CIS, Post Logic, Digiscope did a lot of the work...
...coming up in the next rain sequence.
What's interesting about contemporary filmmaking...
...is it used to be you couldn't remove wires or posts.
So production had to be very careful about what they put into a scene.
Although, I'd like to say this production wasn't bad that way.
On films I've worked on, they've left the food truck in the background.
Because it was quicker to shoot the scene...
...and have the truck removed in postproduction.
And the whole business of wires now, and posts to support people...
...to make them fly and do other things is rampant. Every other shot...
...in action sequences...
...often times have support rods, or tubes...
...or cranes, or people's hands, that you end up going back to paint out.
It's one of the things digital imagery has provided to the filmmakers.
You can take eyes from one take and a mouth from another...
...and paint them together to make another shot.
Pixel Magic was our other vendor, I forgot to mention.
Now it doesn't necessarily mean that you should do that.
We had many wire removal and fix it shots...
...they all just blend together. I can't remember where they all are.

It's kind of an interesting mix of a collage of fighting styles...
...which, I think, gives him a personality all his own.

So New York City, real New York City with all the bits and pieces...
We started using a technique called photogrametry...
...where we were gonna create large chunks of the city...
...based on multiple-positioned camera photographs of New York.
It turned out that that technique didn't provide us enough resolution.
Meaning that the buildings we created that way...
...didn't have enough detail in them for the camera to get close to them.
We then decided instead of doing that...
...to use conventional...
...surveyors' instruments...
...to create a mathematical model of buildings in much higher detail...
...and use still photographs of the surfaces of those buildings to be...
...stretched back onto that geometry.
We had the shape of the building and we had a picture of the surfaces...
...on the building. We placed those surfaces onto the building.
It's called mapping.
And as you look at this... As we continues through this...
...you're gonna see extensive use of this technique.
We had buildings in the middle ground, foreground, and in the distance.
Some were in original photography and some were added in after the fact.
The other weakness photogrametry had was you tend...
...to get the built in lighting of the day...
...so it depends on how overcast it is...
...the sun's angle, what time of day. You don't have leeway.
Our buildings had to hold up for different sun angles...
...and different times of day.
So our main texture painter guy, Carey, ended up taking all the photos.
They painted out a lot of detail that we'd add back in later with lighting.
Also, paint the edges so we could tile...
...and reuse some of that photography to extend buildings.
Talk about the tiles. That's an important thing that we used.
- The tiles in terms of... - Just how we did it.
We started out making this whole building system.
We knew we would have complex scenes that had a lot of geometry.
We made our buildings as building blocks.
We used a system that Steve LeVietes created in Houdini software...
...to easily instance pieces and assemble them all together.
Also there was a notion that when we took these photos...
...they had to seam up on the edges.
If you look at real buildings, there's lots of variation in color...
...and lighting across these buildings.
Our texture painters had to balance those out of the textures...
...make them so they could seam up together and work generically.
The beautiful thing about the tiles is, you get this hemisphere...
...which functions as a plate.
But a normal plate, you've gotta work within what the camera has shot.
When you have this hemisphere of tiles stitched together...
...you can move the camera all around...
...and create your own camera move within this background.
Our pan and tile system...
...which was another technique we used to put behind our CG buildings.
The camera sits in one place. You take a shot and then tilt up.
Take one to the right and left, until you have these...
...photos that define or capture the panorama before you.
If you look at David Hockney's art, you see some of this kind of tiling.
We did it in a much more precise fashion, but far less artistic.
It's a lot of single images which are combined together...
...to create a much larger image at high resolution.
That allows you to move in closer and further away and to pan...
...and move up and down on this big domed projection.
It's a technique that's been getting more and more...
...in use over the last couple of years.
Many years ago, you'd have to assemble it in 3-D and render out.
Nowadays, at Imageworks, we've got a system...
...where we do it in our last compositing stage so it's much faster.
One of the interesting things about visual effects in movies is that...
...you start out with scripts that have more stuff than you can afford to do.
Using resource as a definition of time and money.
You always have twice the material as you have the resource to do.
Throughout the creation of the film, you have to go back...
...and constantly be on top of deciding where you'll put the resource.
We knew we had to create a virtual version of the Spider-Man character...
...a virtual version of the Goblin...
...and a virtual version of portions of New York...
...to allow us to do the kinds of camera moves that we wanted...
...because they weren't allowing anybody to swing through the streets.
So those were our primary allocations of resource.
Then as the script developed, as we worked on the movie...
...the story became clearer to us.
We then had to re-evaluate where we were putting our efforts or resource.
And one of the things that was... We also knew we had to do the web.
The web was a component of resource allocation.
But as we continued through the process...
...we had to re-evaluate what was being put into the film.
We'd get new sequences they wanted to make a story point.
Then that sequence would be evaluated, then abandoned because of expense.
And that was going on for the live-action stuff as well.
It requires that you be very flexible.
You have to be willing to abandon a scene that you like that you've...
...spent months developing since it no longer applies in the story.
That's the hardest thing in doing visual effects and filmmaking...
...is giving away your "babies," so to speak...
...in pursuit of a more effective storytelling.
You also have to be willing to meet...
...an impossible challenge at the last moment. When we did this film...
...it wasn't unusual in the last two or three weeks...
...for the director and editor to come up with a shot that required...
...some heroic performance.
We talked about one of the shots earlier which was Spider-Man...
...swinging through buildings on his way to the fire.
That shot came out through animation, lighting...
...and compositing in record time. You have to be light on your feet...
...and willing to pursue the end goal of the story, first and foremost.
Seems like on every film, you always promise something...
...that you have no idea whether or not you can do.
You have to.
And that's generally how new ways of doing things are created.
What's interesting is that Sam's very pragmatic.
He chose, in many cases, to use practical effects...
...meaning mechanical effects or stunts.
Other filmmakers I've worked with have defaulted...
...to a digital solution immediately.
He'd explore more traditional methods extensively before allocating...
...that visual effects resource to a single shot or a sequence.
And I think that that shows up in his understanding of this story.
He knows where he wants to put the money, the resource...
...in terms of the telling of the story.
Just another note, there's a sort of different class...
...of shot that's become more popular recently...
...and that's these product placement or product replacement shots.
We went through a sequence earlier where he's shooting at a can.
- Of course, we replaced products. - Three different kinds of cans.
We went through different types of cans.
There's a critical phone call shot coming up where Tobey's on the phone...
...and it was a brand of payphone nobody wanted to see in the shot.
So we had one of our vendors take it out.
So there's a lot of attention to those sort of details that happens nowadays.
I think people must think that people will want to...
...drink and eat the same things that Spider-Man does.
- I think so. - I do.
I personally eat almost every sugared cereal that has his picture on it.
How do you think he makes all that web?
You were supposed to be asleep.
You know, you were about 6 years old when M.J.'s family moved in next door.
When she got out of the car, and you saw her for the first time...
...you grabbed me and said, "Aunt May, is that an angel?"
- Did I say that? - You sure did.
Now it'll get fast and furious.
This last portion of the movie goes at about 100 miles an hour.
We'll never be able to talk fast enough.
We should go through all the methods that we were thinking of.
There's a moment when he has to jettison...
...himself to the Queensborough Bridge...
...and he attaches his webs to the top of these flagpoles...
...pulls himself backward and then lets go and springs off.
We went through several ideas on how to get him over there.
From cannons to clock springs, to...
So much of this next sequence is all virtual sets.
It gave us flexibility to explore ideas like that.
The thing that's interesting to me is that...
...we paid very little attention to physics.
The truth is, I'm a stickler for physics.
I like to make stuff kind of stay within the practical world.
That probably is a disadvantage.
Actually, it's probably a good balance...
...because Sam and the writers' penchant...
...is for writing stuff that's so fantastic that you might not buy it.
So I temper that particular tendency on their part by going:
"Physically, this couldn't happen."
So the sequences that you see here, swing from one end of the pole...
...to the other. Things that are practical and that aren't.
But the truth is, it all goes together in a way where you're not aware of it.
I think that the willing suspension of disbelief has been achieved here.
In a couple of these shots, Anthony and I also got little cameos.
- We're people on the ground... - That's my daughter right there.
That's Chloe.
That was a shot by Brad Booker of him looking into the tram.
It was a shot done early on and Sam liked...
...the acting from the Goblin.
Lit by Kevin Hahn. It was one of our first lighting jobs on Goblin.
This is an integration of all manner of mechanical effects too.
We photographed explosions against black so we could...
...combine them in those images.
There are miniatures...
...combined with computer generated cables and cable cars and people.
This is a real potpourri of the visual effects techniques.
We shot from the top of the Queensborough Bridge.
We were gonna climb to the top of the bridge and photograph...
Here's Anthony's shot of him being shot to the bridge with the flagpoles.
Chris Williams, Ken Morissey, Bill Diaz did this great shot of him.
And then James Pam's does the shot where he comes over the top here.
This is a real great shot in the film. It was a beautiful shot.
So this is again... Mechanical effects boys...
...had to provide wind, explosions, fog, smoke and all kinds of things.
So it's another case where departments had to collaborate extensively.
We did lots of photography on Roosevelt Island in New York.
It was exciting to have our own Roosevelt Island tram car.
We rented it. We could make it go back and forth, wherever we wanted to.
What pre-visualization did for us is...
...when you've a sequence where points of view aren't on the same level...
...you have to establish the camera's angle relative to the live action.
Pre-visualization allowed us to say:
The camera needs to be 40 feet below the subject on a 15 millimeter lens.
Those kinds of details allowed us to photograph our characters...
...in some cases, against green screen...
...then put them into a photography of the real bridge or a CGI bridge.
Ken Hahn is the CG supervisor of this...
...and did a great job pulling together all the green screen and CG elements...
...and putting up with listening to me talk about the black levels...
...and different variations across the images.
This is a difficult area, to keep the character doing the same thing...
...all the time so there's a continuity of what he does in the physical world.
You have a temptation to make the character do something human...
...and you can't let yourself fall into that.
Just like before when he was shooting web and going through the bridge.
He comes up, flies over the top and I had him landing so hard...
...in this big follow through.
Sam said, "He can't do that." I said, "He has to do that."
I thought about it and he was right. He's not a person, he's Spider-Man.
As opposed to this, which a person could easily do.
Lots of pieces went into this sequence.
The toughest part is you're working with a large set piece, this bridge.
You get close to the characters, it's hard to see the bridge and vice versa.
So this was a real tour de force of editing and design of sequence...
...to integrate the pieces so you had a sense of the environment...
...but were still close enough to the characters to read their emotions.
It was interesting to just set up all the lighting of our virtual sets...
...and matched pieces. Sometimes it worked, sometimes it didn't.
We just dialed in different fall offs and bright and dark areas for shots.
Spider-Man, watch out!
Interesting thing about this, this is our "hulking ruin" sequence.
Spider-Man gets pretty beat up in this sequence.
We made a full CG version of Spider-Man completely beat up.
One of our guys painted beautiful maps.
It's this pristine CG model that looks fabulous.
And by the end of the cut that they made, it was no longer in there.
I hope in the sequel, there's an area where he's beat up...
...pretty similarly to this so we can use that model. I'm sure we won't.
That was the shot that, because of the rating system...
He had a mouthful of blood spewing out...
...and to get the rating down to PG-13, we had to turn that blood...
...from red to clear to be spit.
We had to take out a bit so it didn't look like he was spewing a fountain.
This was an interesting solution with the web.
This is of course a new kind of web...
...but also had to look like the real web because it was already shot.
Todd Boyce spent a lot of time weaving that pattern...
...making it match the next shot.
This sequence really got out down. At one point, it went on for days.
The thing that happens is these sequences...
...end up being distillations of much, much larger vintages.
The Razorbats used to be in this sequence.
There were Razorbats and even another kind of thing at some point.
In this sequence, there were flocks of them all over the place.
But again, I think that the distillation...
...really focuses on the development of the characters.
That's the saving grace of this movie. You pay more attention...
...to the people involved than you do to the...
- Action. - Action, yeah.
This is definitely an homage to some of Sam's earlier movies.
Army of Darkness or whatever.
A lot of speed up on this thing.
Did that wall move or no?

Again the spider sense comes into play here.
The spider sense thing was a huge combination of many elements.
It's a beautiful digital blade that comes out of this.
It comes out of the glider.
It's photography done on the set, paintwork...
...some CG components that were generated...
...in three dimensions and painted and added. The glider is a virtual...
...version of the glider, a CGI version...
...and that's combined with our...
...actually live actor in the foreground.
Give me your hand.
For the spider sense shot coming up, Dan Kramer did a great job...
...because we shot different ideas, motion control...
...and the end result ended up being a combination of some of those ideas.
So we had to piece together different photography in.
Godspeed, Spider-Man.
That shot right there.

Well, it's back to your faithful servant, the glider...
...hanging out, waiting to take care...
...of the threat.
And of course that sequence was cut down to get into the PG-13 rating.
Some of our work was cut out of there as well.
I'm sure in the director's cut, the R-rated version, that'll show up.
What have you done?!
A lot of these shots have to be leading towards sequels to come.
Setting up some of the situations.
So this is the set-up before the final shot...
...in which Sam had a small request...
...that the animation in this last shot...
...be more incredible, more astounding.
The all time, best animated shot in history. That was all he asked for.
We went away and tried to do that. This went through many generations.
But it was interesting to see...
...as we got down I think John has said before, what is it?
Ninety percent of your time you spend getting ready for the shot...
...and 10 percent getting it done.
All the camera moves and the animation.
There were minute adjustments on these things.
One frame of a change of a move where the camera...
...would come close to a building or move.
It really got down to a beautiful shot that I think is well remembered...
...in great animation by Peter Giliberti.
Lighting and compositing-wise it came down to the wire.
Like, our artists Marc Rienzo, Jeff Stern, Dan Abrams...
...Laurence, Dan Eaton all these guys in the last week...
...working around the clock, sleeping under their desks.
It's funny, the president of our company, Tim Sarnoff...
...had to come and force these guys to go home and walk them out.
We had people drive them home so they wouldn't crash.
That's how dedicated they were to this last shot.
It took about seven months for this last shot.
When you watch this shot...
...I mean, if you've seen the movie in a theatre where it's on a big screen...
...it actually succeeds in getting you to sort of be physically involved...
...in swinging through the city. In terms of a storytelling component...
...it gives you a sense of what it's like to be Spider-Man.
Not the irony where he has to deny the woman of his dreams...
...but where you understand why he goes forward.
Why he sees some kind of real future...
...in the business of being responsible for this power he wields.
It comes along with some great fun too.
And I think that this shot embodies all of those things.
It embodies the best of the work of the visual effects in the movie.

In the performance of the character, in the beginning...
...he's not sure what move to make, where to shoot his web...
...how to move around it. And through the film he develops.
Whereas in this shot, you feel that he knows where he's going...
...and exactly what he's doing and he's in control of his movement.
I think a lot of the photography ended up helping us out in the last shot.
The photography that we got on that Sunday in downtown L.A. swinging...
...through the cars and everything built off of that.
And that stuff you got in New York for the tiles. The sort of...
...golden-hour looking tiles that we processed and pushed more.
That sort of grounded it and gave us a base to build our virtual environment.
Also we had good success with finishing the buildings off...
...and figuring out how to use them in the lighting...
...environment to make them feel more real. So when you...
...get to the point of watching...
...what he's doing and what's happening around him...
...it's very spectacular.
The composition, the choice of buildings...
...the color compositing. Even if it were something that were...
...done for real and wasn't put together as elements...
